inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution\"\n     4→category: development\n     5→complexity: high\n     6→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Skill\n     7→mcp-servers: [sequential, serena, context7]\n     8→personas: [architect, analyzer, backend]\n     9→version: \"1.0.0\"\n    10→---\n    11→\n    12→# /sc:cli-portify\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows (commands + skills + agents) into programmatic CLI pipelines with deterministic control flow, formal artifact validation, and live monitoring.\n    15→\n    16→## Triggers\n    17→\n    18→When the user requests workflow portification, CLI pipeline conversion, or programmatic workflow generation from an existing skill.\n    19→\n    20→## Usage\n    21→\n    22→```\n    23→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>] [--dry-run] [--skip-integration]\n    24→```\n    25→\n    26→Both `@file` syntax and explicit file paths are supported for `--workflow`.\n    27→\n    28→## Arguments\n    29→\n    30→| Argument | Required | Default | Description |\n    31→|----------|----------|---------|-------------|\n    32→| `--workflow` | Yes | -- | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify. |\n    33→| `--name` | No | Derived from workflow name | CLI subcommand name (kebab-case). |\n    34→| `--output` | No | `src/superclaude/cli/<derived-name>/` | Output directory for the generated CLI module. |\n    35→| `--dry-run` | No | false | Execute Phases 0-2 only — emit contracts, no code generation. |\n    36→| `--skip-integration` | No | false | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring (Phase 4). |\n    37→\n    38→### Name Derivation\n    39→\n    40→When `--name` is not provided, derive from the workflow:\n    41→1. Strip `sc-` prefix if present\n    42→2. Strip `-protocol` suffix if present\n    43→3. Convert to kebab-case for CLI, snake_case for Python modules\n    44→\n    45→## Input Validation\n    46→\n    47→Before invoking the skill, validate all inputs. On any validation failure, emit an error and exit without invoking the skill.\n    48→\n    49→**Error format:**\n    50→```\n    51→error_code: <ERROR_CODE>\n    52→message: <human-readable description with corrective action>\n    53→```\n    54→\n    55→**Validation checks:**\n    56→\n    57→1. **Workflow argument present**: `--workflow` must be provided.\n    58→   - Error: `error_code: MISSING_WORKFLOW` / `message: \"--workflow is required. Provide a skill directory path or sc-* name.\"`\n    59→\n    60→2. **Workflow path resolves**: The workflow path resolves to a directory containing `SKILL.md`.\n    61→   - Error: `error_code: INVALID_PATH` / `message: \"Path '<path>' does not exist or does not contain a SKILL.md file.\"`\n    62→\n    63→3. **Workflow path unambiguous**: Only one candidate directory matches the workflow name.\n    64→   - Error: `error_code: AMBIGUOUS_PATH` / `message: \"Multiple candidates found for '<name>': <list>. Use a full path to disambiguate.\"`\n    65→\n    66→4. **Output directory writable**: If `--output` is provided, its parent directory exists and is writable.\n    67→   - Error: `error_code: OUTPUT_NOT_WRITABLE` / `message: \"Output directory parent '<parent>' does not exist or is not writable.\"`\n    68→\n    69→5. **No name collision**: The derived or provided `--name` does not collide with an existing non-portified CLI module.\n    70→   - Error: `error_code: NAME_COLLISION` / `message: \"CLI module '<name>' already exists at '<path>' and was not generated by portification. Use --name to specify an alternative.\"`\n    71→\n    72→6. **Name derivation succeeds**: If `--name` is not provided, derivation from the workflow name produces a valid identifier.\n    73→   - Error: `error_code: DERIVATION_FAILED` / `message: \"Could not derive CLI name from workflow '<name>'. Use --name to specify explicitly.\"`\n    74→\n    75→## Activation\n    76→\n    77→**Classification**: STRICT -- multi-file code generation operation with API conformance requirements.\n    78→\n    79→**MANDATORY**: Before executing any protocol steps, invoke:\n    80→> Skill sc:cli-portify-protocol\n    81→\n    82→Pass the following context:\n    83→- Workflow path: resolved path to the skill directory\n    84→- CLI name: resolved name (derived or from --name)\n    85→- Output directory: resolved output path\n    86→- Dry run: boolean flag value\n    87→- Skip integration: boolean flag value\n    88→\n    89→Do NOT attempt to execute portification using only this command file.\n    90→The full portification protocol is in the protocol skill.\n    91→\n    92→## Examples\n    93→\n    94→```bash\n    95→# Basic: portify a skill\n    96→/sc:cli-portify --workflow src/superclaude/skills/sc-cleanup-audit/\n    97→\n    98→# With explicit name\n    99→/sc:cli-portify --workflow sc-cleanup-audit --name cleanup-audit\n   100→\n   101→# Dry run (analysis only, no code generation)\n   102→/sc:cli-portify --workflow sc-adversarial --dry-run\n   103→\n   104→# Skip main.py integration\n   105→/sc:cli-portify --workflow sc-roadmap --skip-integration\n   106→\n   107→# Full invocation\n   108→/sc:cli-portify --workflow src/superclaude/skills/sc-tasklist-protocol/ --name tasklist --output src/superclaude/cli/tasklist/\n   109→```\n   110→\n   111→## Boundaries\n   112→\n   113→**Will:**\n   114→- Parse and validate input arguments\n   115→- Derive CLI name from workflow name\n   116→- Resolve workflow path to a skill directory\n   117→- Invoke the protocol skill with validated context\n   118→- Report generated file paths on completion\n   119→\n   120→**Will Not:**\n   121→- Execute the portification protocol (that is the skill's job)\n   122→- Modify source skill files\n   123→- Run the generated CLI pipeline\n   124→- Generate anything beyond what the protocol produces\n   125→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01MkLQv4vC6oFgW5qdoLhxSH","session_id":"b2ef0efa-f51b-45bb-8b9b-bca7a80178ed","uuid":"a2c89e7a-2013-4a5f-bdf0-ecb29ebf9903"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_hynYosXCqRdLyeqASWrhvy4g","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---\n     2→name: sc-cli-portify-protocol\n     3→description: \"Full behavioral protocol for sc:cli-portify — port inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution.\"\n     4→category: development\n     5→complexity: high\n     6→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Task\n     7→mcp-servers: [sequential, serena, context7, auggie-mcp]\n     8→personas: [architect, analyzer, backend]\n     9→argument-hint: \"--workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>] [--dry-run] [--skip-integration]\"\n    10→---\n    11→\n    12→# sc:cli-portify — Workflow-to-CLI Pipeline Compiler\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines that ensure 100% completion and consistency between runs.\n    15→\n    16→## Purpose\n    17→\n    18→SuperClaude workflows implemented as commands + skills + agents + refs rely entirely on Claude's reasoning for orchestration. This works well for flexible, one-off tasks but breaks down for repeatable multi-step pipelines where you need:\n    19→\n    20→- **Deterministic control flow** — Python decides what runs when, not inference\n    21→- **Formal artifact validation** — Gates check output quality programmatically\n    22→- **Resume/retry** — Failed steps resume without re-running everything\n    23→- **Live monitoring** — TUI shows progress, stall detection, diagnostics\n    24→- **Budget economics** — Turn ledger tracks consumption across subprocesses\n    25→- **Consistent behavior** — Same inputs produce structurally identical outputs every run\n    26→\n    27→The existing `pipeline/` and `sprint/` modules already solve these problems. This skill bridges the gap by analyzing a workflow and generating a new CLI module that uses these proven patterns.\n    28→\n    29→## What Gets Generated\n    30→\n    31→A CLI subcommand package under `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`:\n    32→\n    33→| File | Purpose | When Generated |\n    34→|------|---------|----------------|\n    35→| `models.py` | Domain types extending `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult` | Always |\n    36→| `gates.py` | `GateCriteria` + semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]` | Always |\n    37→| `prompts.py` | LLM contract builders per Claude-assisted step | Always |\n    38→| `config.py` | CLI arg resolution, file discovery, config construction | Always |\n    39→| `executor.py` | Synchronous supervisor loop with `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism | Always |\n    40→| `monitor.py` | NDJSON incremental output parser with domain signals | Always |\n    41→| `process.py` | `ClaudeProcess` subclass with domain-specific prompt building | Always |\n    42→| `tui.py` | Rich live dashboard with gate state machine | Always |\n    43→| `logging_.py` | Dual JSONL + Markdown execution logging | Always |\n    44→| `diagnostics.py` | `DiagnosticCollector`, `FailureClassifier`, `ReportGenerator` | Always |\n    45→| `commands.py` | Click CLI group and subcommands | Always |\n    46→| `__init__.py` | Package exports | Always |\n    47→| `inventory.py` | Pure-programmatic file discovery/classification | If workflow has discovery steps |\n    48→| `filtering.py` | Pure-programmatic inter-step data filtering | If workflow has filtering between steps |\n    49→\n    50→Plus integration patches for `main.py`.\n    51→\n    52→## Required Input\n    53→\n    54→```\n    55→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>]\n    56→```\n    57→\n    58→| Argument | Required | Description |\n    59→|----------|----------|-------------|\n    60→| `--workflow` | Yes | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify |\n    61→| `--name` | No | CLI subcommand name (default: derived from workflow) |\n    62→| `--output` | No | Output directory (default: `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`) |\n    63→| `--dry-run` | No | Show decomposition plan without generating code |\n    64→| `--skip-integration` | No | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring |\n    65→\n    66→**STOP** if `--workflow` is not provided or the path doesn't resolve to a valid skill.\n    67→\n    68→## Behavioral Flow\n    69→\n    70→Four phases, each producing artifacts that feed the next. Present output to user for review after Phases 1 and 2 before continuing.\n    71→\n    72→### Phase 1: Workflow Analysis\n    73→\n    74→Load `refs/analysis-protocol.md` before this phase. It contains the discovery checklist, step decomposition algorithm, classification rubric, and output format template.\n    75→\n    76→**Goal**: Decompose the target workflow into a structured pipeline specification.\n    77→\n    78→1. **Discover components** — Find the command `.md`, skill `SKILL.md`, all `refs/` and `rules/`, all `templates/`, all `scripts/`, and all referenced agents. Build a component inventory table with paths, line counts, and purposes.\n    79→\n    80→2. **Map the protocol** — Extract the step-by-step behavioral flow. Pay attention to:\n    81→   - Wave/phase boundaries in the skill\n    82→   - Agent delegation patterns (which agent, parallel vs sequential)\n    83→   - Conditional execution paths (flags that skip steps)\n    84→   - Implicit inter-step data flow (what one step produces that another consumes)\n    85→\n    86→3. **Identify steps** — A new Step boundary exists when:\n    87→   - A new artifact is produced\n    88→   - A different agent takes over\n    89→   - Execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    90→   - A quality gate must be evaluated\n    91→   - Operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    92→\n    93→4. **Classify each step** on the programmatic spectrum:\n    94→   - **Pure programmatic**: Runs as a Python function, no Claude subprocess. Examples: file inventory via `git ls-files`, config construction, file filtering between passes, scoring formulas, structural validation, batch assignment. If the logic is deterministic and the input/output formats are well-defined, it should be programmatic.\n    95→   - **Claude-assisted**: Runs as a Claude subprocess with a prompt contract and gate. Examples: content analysis, writing reports, rubric-based assessment, creative synthesis, debate.\n    96→   - **Hybrid**: Programmatic setup → Claude subprocess → programmatic validation. Examples: steps that need Claude for content but have strict structural validation on the output.\n    97→\n    98→5. **Map dependencies and parallel groups** — Draw the data flow: which steps produce artifacts consumed by downstream steps? Which steps are independent and can run concurrently? For batched workflows, identify where batch parallelism applies.\n    99→\n   100→6. **Extract gates** — For each step output, identify what validation the original workflow expects. Map these to gate tiers: EXEMPT (informational), LIGHT (exists + non-empty), STANDARD (structural checks), STRICT (structural + semantic).\n   101→\n   102→7. **Assign gate modes** — `GateMode.BLOCKING` for steps whose output feeds downstream steps. `GateMode.TRAILING` for quality-only checks that don't affect data flow.\n   103→\n   104→**Output**: `portify-analysis.md` — Complete decomposition following the template in `refs/analysis-protocol.md`. Must include: component inventory, step graph with classifications, parallel groups, gates summary, data flow diagram, and recommendations.\n   105→\n   106→**Present to user for review before Phase 2.**\n   107→\n   108→### Phase 2: Pipeline Specification\n   109→\n   110→Load `refs/pipeline-spec.md` before this phase. It contains Step definition patterns, model design patterns, gate patterns, executor patterns, and the new unified-audit-gating patterns (TurnLedger, gate modes, subprocess isolation, context injection, resume semantics).\n   111→\n   112→**Goal**: Convert the analysis into concrete, code-ready specifications.\n   113→\n   114→1. **Design the Step graph** — Map each workflow step to `Step` objects. Batched parallel steps are `list[Step]` groups. Dynamic step counts (e.g., batch count determined at runtime) require a `build_steps()` function.\n   115→\n   116→2. **Define models** — Design domain-specific dataclasses:\n   117→   - Config extending `PipelineConfig` with workflow-specific fields (source paths, pass selection, batch sizes, stall/timeout policies)\n   118→   - Status enum with domain-specific states (e.g., `VALIDATION_FAIL`, `CRITICAL_FAIL` for audit workflows)\n   119→   - Result extending `StepResult` with execution telemetry\n   120→   - Aggregate result with `resume_command()` and `suggested_resume_budget`\n   121→   - Monitor state with domain-specific NDJSON signals\n   122→   - `TurnLedger` integration for budget tracking\n   123→\n   124→3. **Design prompts** — For each Claude-assisted step, write the prompt builder. Each prompt must specify:\n   125→   - What input files/context to embed (inline for <50KB, `--file` args for larger)\n   126→   - Required output sections and frontmatter fields\n   127→   - Machine-readable markers: `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT`\n   128→   - Any structural requirements the gate will check\n   129→\n   130→   If prompts collectively exceed ~300 lines, put them in a separate `portify-prompts.md`.\n   131→\n   132→4. **Design gates** — For each output artifact, define:\n   133→   - `GateCriteria` with tier, required frontmatter, min lines, semantic checks\n   134→   - `GateMode` (BLOCKING or TRAILING)\n   135→   - All semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]`\n   136→\n   137→5. **Implement pure-programmatic steps** — Write actual runnable Python code for steps classified as \"pure programmatic\". These run as direct function calls in the executor, bypassing `ClaudeProcess`. Include: file discovery, domain classification, batch assignment, inter-pass filtering, config construction.\n   138→\n   139→6. **Design the executor** — Sprint-style synchronous supervisor:\n   140→   - `ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism (NOT async/await)\n   141→   - `time.sleep()` polling loops for monitoring\n   142→   - Per-step/batch monitoring with stall detection\n   143→   - `TurnLedger` budget guards before each launch\n   144→   - Runner-authored truth: reports from observed data, not Claude self-reporting\n   145→   - Subprocess isolation (4-layer model) for child Claude sessions\n   146→   - Context injection: prior results compressed into subsequent prompts\n   147→   - Signal-aware shutdown with `SignalHandler`\n   148→\n   149→7. **Plan integration** — Click command group, main.py import, file generation order.\n   150→\n   151→**Output**: `portify-spec.md` (+ optional `portify-prompts.md`) — Complete code spec with models, gates, executor design, pure-programmatic implementations, and integration plan.\n   152→\n   153→**Present to user for approval before Phase 3.**\n   154→\n   155→### Phase 3: Code Generation\n   156→\n   157→Load `refs/code-templates.md` before this phase. It contains parameterized file templates for every module.\n   158→\n   159→Generate files in dependency order (models → gates → prompts → config → monitor → process → executor → tui → logging → diagnostics → commands → __init__). Each file imports from `superclaude.cli.pipeline` for shared base types and follows sprint/roadmap naming conventions.\n   160→\n   161→### Phase 4: Integration\n   162→\n   163→1. Patch `main.py` — Add import and `app.add_command()`\n   164→2. Verify imports — Quick check for circular dependencies\n   165→3. Generate structural test — Validates step graph, gate definitions, and model consistency\n   166→4. Write `portify-summary.md` — File inventory, CLI usage, step graph, known limitations\n   167→\n   168→## Decision Framework: What Becomes Programmatic\n   169→\n   170→The value of portification is moving orchestration out of inference. Here are concrete examples from tested workflows:\n   171→\n   172→| Operation | Classification | Why |\n   173→|-----------|---------------|-----|\n   174→| Enumerate repo files | Pure programmatic | `git ls-files` + pattern matching is deterministic |\n   175→| Classify file domains | Pure programmatic | Extension/path pattern rules, no judgment needed |\n   176→| Assign files to batches | Pure programmatic | Math: divide files by batch_size, assign to agents |\n   177→| Filter Pass 1 results for Pass 2 | Pure programmatic | Parse markdown, extract classifications, filter list |\n   178→| Analyze file for issues | Claude-assisted | Requires reading code, understanding context |\n   179→| Score variant quality | Hybrid | Formulas are programmatic, rubric evaluation is Claude |\n   180→| Validate structural integrity | Pure programmatic | Regex checks, section presence, field counts |\n   181→| Spot-check verification | Claude-assisted | Re-reads files, re-evaluates claims independently |\n   182→| Merge batch reports | Claude-assisted | Requires synthesis, deduplication, pattern extraction |\n   183→| Compute convergence score | Pure programmatic | `agreed / total` — arithmetic |\n   184→| Generate executive summary | Claude-assisted | Requires narrative synthesis |\n   185→\n   186→### Rule of Thumb\n   187→If you can write a Python function for it with clear input→output types and no ambiguity, make it programmatic. If it requires reading natural language, making judgments, or synthesizing information, use Claude with a gate to verify the output.\n   188→\n   189→## Code Generation Principles\n   190→\n   191→These align with the unified-audit-gating architecture in `pipeline/`.\n   192→\n   193→### Critical Constraints\n   194→1. **Synchronous execution** — Sprint uses `threading` + `time.sleep()` polling. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism. Do NOT use async/await.\n   195→2. **Gate function signatures** — All semantic checks return `tuple[bool, str]`. The reason string feeds diagnostics and retry decisions.\n   196→3. **Runner-authored truth** — Reports derive from runner-observed data (exit codes, artifacts, gates, monitor signals), not Claude's self-reported status.\n   197→4. **Deterministic flow control** — Python makes all decisions about what runs next. Claude never decides \"what's next\".\n   198→\n   199→### Shared Pipeline Patterns\n   200→5. **Reuse `pipeline/` primitives** — `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult`, `GateMode`, `GateCriteria`, `SemanticCheck`, `gate_passed()`, `ClaudeProcess`\n   201→6. **Gate modes** — `BLOCKING` (default) stops pipeline on failure. `TRAILING` defers evaluation. Use trailing only for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n   202→7. **Turn budget** — `TurnLedger` with debit/credit/guard for multi-subprocess pipelines. Pre-launch guards prevent launching when budget too low.\n   203→8. **Subprocess isolation** — 4-layer model: scoped work dir, git ceiling, isolated plugin dir, isolated settings dir.\n   204→9. **Context injection** — Prior results serialized into subsequent prompts. Compress older, preserve recent.\n   205→10. **Resume-first failures** — On halt: exact resume command, remaining work, diagnostic path, budget suggestion.\n   206→11. **Diagnostic chain** — Runner-side failure analysis that doesn't consume turn budget.\n   207→\n   208→### Output Guidance\n   209→- `portify-analysis.md`: Under 400 lines. Step graph, gates, data flow.\n   210→- `portify-spec.md`: Split prompts to separate file if spec exceeds 800 lines.\n   211→- Pure-programmatic steps: Full implementation code, not descriptions.\n   212→\n   213→## Boundaries\n   214→\n   215→### Will Do\n   216→- Analyze any SuperClaude skill/command/agent workflow\n   217→- Decompose into structured pipeline specification\n   218→- Generate complete CLI subcommand module\n   219→- Wire module into CLI infrastructure\n   220→- Generate structural tests for pipeline\n   221→\n   222→### Will Not Do\n   223→- Execute the generated pipeline\n   224→- Modify original skill/command/agent files\n   225→- Create new skills or agents\n   226→- Make architectural decisions about the workflow's logic\n   227→- Generate LLM content quality tests (only structural)\n   228→\n   229→## Related Commands\n   230→\n   231→- `/sc:implement` — General feature implementation\n   232→- `/sc:design` — System architecture design\n   233→- `/sc:analyze` — Code analysis for understanding workflows\n   234→- `/sc:build` — Build and compile after generating\n   235→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01MkLQv4vC6oFgW5qdoLhxSH","session_id":"b2ef0efa-f51b-45bb-8b9b-bca7a80178ed","uuid":"4b7b492f-c8e0-4251-8d31-681f4b1b4256"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_gbrRLLFS4bEHIrWsaUMFezM8","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---\n     2→name: sc-cli-portify\n     3→description: \"Port inference-based SuperClaude workflows (commands + skills + agents) into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution. Use this skill when you need to convert any multi-step workflow into a reliable, repeatable CLI runner that uses Python to control flow and Claude to fill in content. Triggers on: portify, CLI pipeline, programmatic workflow, convert skill to CLI, workflow runner, deterministic pipeline, move to code, make repeatable, ensure consistency.\"\n     4→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Task\n     5→---\n     6→\n     7→> **DEPRECATED**: This skill has been replaced by `sc-cli-portify-protocol/`.\n     8→> The protocol skill contains the full behavioral protocol with updated API-aligned refs.\n     9→> This directory is scheduled for removal after Phase 5 validation.\n    10→> Do NOT use this skill for new work — use `Skill sc:cli-portify-protocol` instead.\n    11→\n    12→# sc:cli-portify — Workflow-to-CLI Pipeline Compiler\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines that ensure 100% completion and consistency between runs.\n    15→\n    16→## Purpose\n    17→\n    18→SuperClaude workflows implemented as commands + skills + agents + refs rely entirely on Claude's reasoning for orchestration. This works well for flexible, one-off tasks but breaks down for repeatable multi-step pipelines where you need:\n    19→\n    20→- **Deterministic control flow** — Python decides what runs when, not inference\n    21→- **Formal artifact validation** — Gates check output quality programmatically\n    22→- **Resume/retry** — Failed steps resume without re-running everything\n    23→- **Live monitoring** — TUI shows progress, stall detection, diagnostics\n    24→- **Budget economics** — Turn ledger tracks consumption across subprocesses\n    25→- **Consistent behavior** — Same inputs produce structurally identical outputs every run\n    26→\n    27→The existing `pipeline/` and `sprint/` modules already solve these problems. This skill bridges the gap by analyzing a workflow and generating a new CLI module that uses these proven patterns.\n    28→\n    29→## What Gets Generated\n    30→\n    31→A CLI subcommand package under `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`:\n    32→\n    33→| File | Purpose | When Generated |\n    34→|------|---------|----------------|\n    35→| `models.py` | Domain types extending `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult` | Always |\n    36→| `gates.py` | `GateCriteria` + semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]` | Always |\n    37→| `prompts.py` | LLM contract builders per Claude-assisted step | Always |\n    38→| `config.py` | CLI arg resolution, file discovery, config construction | Always |\n    39→| `executor.py` | Synchronous supervisor loop with `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism | Always |\n    40→| `monitor.py` | NDJSON incremental output parser with domain signals | Always |\n    41→| `process.py` | `ClaudeProcess` subclass with domain-specific prompt building | Always |\n    42→| `tui.py` | Rich live dashboard with gate state machine | Always |\n    43→| `logging_.py` | Dual JSONL + Markdown execution logging | Always |\n    44→| `diagnostics.py` | `DiagnosticCollector`, `FailureClassifier`, `ReportGenerator` | Always |\n    45→| `commands.py` | Click CLI group and subcommands | Always |\n    46→| `__init__.py` | Package exports | Always |\n    47→| `inventory.py` | Pure-programmatic file discovery/classification | If workflow has discovery steps |\n    48→| `filtering.py` | Pure-programmatic inter-step data filtering | If workflow has filtering between steps |\n    49→\n    50→Plus integration patches for `main.py`.\n    51→\n    52→## Required Input\n    53→\n    54→```\n    55→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>]\n    56→```\n    57→\n    58→| Argument | Required | Description |\n    59→|----------|----------|-------------|\n    60→| `--workflow` | Yes | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify |\n    61→| `--name` | No | CLI subcommand name (default: derived from workflow) |\n    62→| `--output` | No | Output directory (default: `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`) |\n    63→| `--dry-run` | No | Show decomposition plan without generating code |\n    64→| `--skip-integration` | No | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring |\n    65→\n    66→**STOP** if `--workflow` is not provided or the path doesn't resolve to a valid skill.\n    67→\n    68→## Behavioral Flow\n    69→\n    70→Four phases, each producing artifacts that feed the next. Present output to user for review after Phases 1 and 2 before continuing.\n    71→\n    72→### Phase 1: Workflow Analysis\n    73→\n    74→Load `refs/analysis-protocol.md` before this phase. It contains the discovery checklist, step decomposition algorithm, classification rubric, and output format template.\n    75→\n    76→**Goal**: Decompose the target workflow into a structured pipeline specification.\n    77→\n    78→1. **Discover components** — Find the command `.md`, skill `SKILL.md`, all `refs/` and `rules/`, all `templates/`, all `scripts/`, and all referenced agents. Build a component inventory table with paths, line counts, and purposes.\n    79→\n    80→2. **Map the protocol** — Extract the step-by-step behavioral flow. Pay attention to:\n    81→   - Wave/phase boundaries in the skill\n    82→   - Agent delegation patterns (which agent, parallel vs sequential)\n    83→   - Conditional execution paths (flags that skip steps)\n    84→   - Implicit inter-step data flow (what one step produces that another consumes)\n    85→\n    86→3. **Identify steps** — A new Step boundary exists when:\n    87→   - A new artifact is produced\n    88→   - A different agent takes over\n    89→   - Execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    90→   - A quality gate must be evaluated\n    91→   - Operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    92→\n    93→4. **Classify each step** on the programmatic spectrum:\n    94→   - **Pure programmatic**: Runs as a Python function, no Claude subprocess. Examples: file inventory via `git ls-files`, config construction, file filtering between passes, scoring formulas, structural validation, batch assignment. If the logic is deterministic and the input/output formats are well-defined, it should be programmatic.\n    95→   - **Claude-assisted**: Runs as a Claude subprocess with a prompt contract and gate. Examples: content analysis, writing reports, rubric-based assessment, creative synthesis, debate.\n    96→   - **Hybrid**: Programmatic setup → Claude subprocess → programmatic validation. Examples: steps that need Claude for content but have strict structural validation on the output.\n    97→\n    98→5. **Map dependencies and parallel groups** — Draw the data flow: which steps produce artifacts consumed by downstream steps? Which steps are independent and can run concurrently? For batched workflows, identify where batch parallelism applies.\n    99→\n   100→6. **Extract gates** — For each step output, identify what validation the original workflow expects. Map these to gate tiers: EXEMPT (informational), LIGHT (exists + non-empty), STANDARD (structural checks), STRICT (structural + semantic).\n   101→\n   102→7. **Assign gate modes** — `GateMode.BLOCKING` for steps whose output feeds downstream steps. `GateMode.TRAILING` for quality-only checks that don't affect data flow.\n   103→\n   104→**Output**: `portify-analysis.md` — Complete decomposition following the template in `refs/analysis-protocol.md`. Must include: component inventory, step graph with classifications, parallel groups, gates summary, data flow diagram, and recommendations.\n   105→\n   106→**Present to user for review before Phase 2.**\n   107→\n   108→### Phase 2: Pipeline Specification\n   109→\n   110→Load `refs/pipeline-spec.md` before this phase. It contains Step definition patterns, model design patterns, gate patterns, executor patterns, and the new unified-audit-gating patterns (TurnLedger, gate modes, subprocess isolation, context injection, resume semantics).\n   111→\n   112→**Goal**: Convert the analysis into concrete, code-ready specifications.\n   113→\n   114→1. **Design the Step graph** — Map each workflow step to `Step` objects. Batched parallel steps are `list[Step]` groups. Dynamic step counts (e.g., batch count determined at runtime) require a `build_steps()` function.\n   115→\n   116→2. **Define models** — Design domain-specific dataclasses:\n   117→   - Config extending `PipelineConfig` with workflow-specific fields (source paths, pass selection, batch sizes, stall/timeout policies)\n   118→   - Status enum with domain-specific states (e.g., `VALIDATION_FAIL`, `CRITICAL_FAIL` for audit workflows)\n   119→   - Result extending `StepResult` with execution telemetry\n   120→   - Aggregate result with `resume_command()` and `suggested_resume_budget`\n   121→   - Monitor state with domain-specific NDJSON signals\n   122→   - `TurnLedger` integration for budget tracking\n   123→\n   124→3. **Design prompts** — For each Claude-assisted step, write the prompt builder. Each prompt must specify:\n   125→   - What input files/context to embed (inline for <50KB, `--file` args for larger)\n   126→   - Required output sections and frontmatter fields\n   127→   - Machine-readable markers: `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT`\n   128→   - Any structural requirements the gate will check\n   129→\n   130→   If prompts collectively exceed ~300 lines, put them in a separate `portify-prompts.md`.\n   131→\n   132→4. **Design gates** — For each output artifact, define:\n   133→   - `GateCriteria` with tier, required frontmatter, min lines, semantic checks\n   134→   - `GateMode` (BLOCKING or TRAILING)\n   135→   - All semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]`\n   136→\n   137→5. **Implement pure-programmatic steps** — Write actual runnable Python code for steps classified as \"pure programmatic\". These run as direct function calls in the executor, bypassing `ClaudeProcess`. Include: file discovery, domain classification, batch assignment, inter-pass filtering, config construction.\n   138→\n   139→6. **Design the executor** — Sprint-style synchronous supervisor:\n   140→   - `ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism (NOT async/await)\n   141→   - `time.sleep()` polling loops for monitoring\n   142→   - Per-step/batch monitoring with stall detection\n   143→   - `TurnLedger` budget guards before each launch\n   144→   - Runner-authored truth: reports from observed data, not Claude self-reporting\n   145→   - Subprocess isolation (4-layer model) for child Claude sessions\n   146→   - Context injection: prior results compressed into subsequent prompts\n   147→   - Signal-aware shutdown with `SignalHandler`\n   148→\n   149→7. **Plan integration** — Click command group, main.py import, file generation order.\n   150→\n   151→**Output**: `portify-spec.md` (+ optional `portify-prompts.md`) — Complete code spec with models, gates, executor design, pure-programmatic implementations, and integration plan.\n   152→\n   153→**Present to user for approval before Phase 3.**\n   154→\n   155→### Phase 3: Code Generation\n   156→\n   157→Load `refs/code-templates.md` before this phase. It contains parameterized file templates for every module.\n   158→\n   159→Generate files in dependency order (models → gates → prompts → config → monitor → process → executor → tui → logging → diagnostics → commands → __init__). Each file imports from `superclaude.cli.pipeline` for shared base types and follows sprint/roadmap naming conventions.\n   160→\n   161→### Phase 4: Integration\n   162→\n   163→1. Patch `main.py` — Add import and `app.add_command()`\n   164→2. Verify imports — Quick check for circular dependencies\n   165→3. Generate structural test — Validates step graph, gate definitions, and model consistency\n   166→4. Write `portify-summary.md` — File inventory, CLI usage, step graph, known limitations\n   167→\n   168→## Decision Framework: What Becomes Programmatic\n   169→\n   170→The value of portification is moving orchestration out of inference. Here are concrete examples from tested workflows:\n   171→\n   172→| Operation | Classification | Why |\n   173→|-----------|---------------|-----|\n   174→| Enumerate repo files | Pure programmatic | `git ls-files` + pattern matching is deterministic |\n   175→| Classify file domains | Pure programmatic | Extension/path pattern rules, no judgment needed |\n   176→| Assign files to batches | Pure programmatic | Math: divide files by batch_size, assign to agents |\n   177→| Filter Pass 1 results for Pass 2 | Pure programmatic | Parse markdown, extract classifications, filter list |\n   178→| Analyze file for issues | Claude-assisted | Requires reading code, understanding context |\n   179→| Score variant quality | Hybrid | Formulas are programmatic, rubric evaluation is Claude |\n   180→| Validate structural integrity | Pure programmatic | Regex checks, section presence, field counts |\n   181→| Spot-check verification | Claude-assisted | Re-reads files, re-evaluates claims independently |\n   182→| Merge batch reports | Claude-assisted | Requires synthesis, deduplication, pattern extraction |\n   183→| Compute convergence score | Pure programmatic | `agreed / total` — arithmetic |\n   184→| Generate executive summary | Claude-assisted | Requires narrative synthesis |\n   185→\n   186→### Rule of Thumb\n   187→If you can write a Python function for it with clear input→output types and no ambiguity, make it programmatic. If it requires reading natural language, making judgments, or synthesizing information, use Claude with a gate to verify the output.\n   188→\n   189→## Code Generation Principles\n   190→\n   191→These align with the unified-audit-gating architecture in `pipeline/`.\n   192→\n   193→### Critical Constraints\n   194→1. **Synchronous execution** — Sprint uses `threading` + `time.sleep()` polling. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism. Do NOT use async/await.\n   195→2. **Gate function signatures** — All semantic checks return `tuple[bool, str]`. The reason string feeds diagnostics and retry decisions.\n   196→3. **Runner-authored truth** — Reports derive from runner-observed data (exit codes, artifacts, gates, monitor signals), not Claude's self-reported status.\n   197→4. **Deterministic flow control** — Python makes all decisions about what runs next. Claude never decides \"what's next\".\n   198→\n   199→### Shared Pipeline Patterns\n   200→5. **Reuse `pipeline/` primitives** — `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult`, `GateMode`, `GateCriteria`, `SemanticCheck`, `gate_passed()`, `ClaudeProcess`\n   201→6. **Gate modes** — `BLOCKING` (default) stops pipeline on failure. `TRAILING` defers evaluation. Use trailing only for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n   202→7. **Turn budget** — `TurnLedger` with debit/credit/guard for multi-subprocess pipelines. Pre-launch guards prevent launching when budget too low.\n   203→8. **Subprocess isolation** — 4-layer model: scoped work dir, git ceiling, isolated plugin dir, isolated settings dir.\n   204→9. **Context injection** — Prior results serialized into subsequent prompts. Compress older, preserve recent.\n   205→10. **Resume-first failures** — On halt: exact resume command, remaining work, diagnostic path, budget suggestion.\n   206→11. **Diagnostic chain** — Runner-side failure analysis that doesn't consume turn budget.\n   207→\n   208→### Output Guidance\n   209→- `portify-analysis.md`: Under 400 lines. Step graph, gates, data flow.\n   210→- `portify-spec.md`: Split prompts to separate file if spec exceeds 800 lines.\n   211→- Pure-programmatic steps: Full implementation code, not descriptions.\n   212→\n   213→## Boundaries\n   214→\n   215→### Will Do\n   216→- Analyze any SuperClaude skill/command/agent workflow\n   217→- Decompose into structured pipeline specification\n   218→- Generate complete CLI subcommand module\n   219→- Wire module into CLI infrastructure\n   220→- Generate structural tests for pipeline\n   221→\n   222→### Will Not Do\n   223→- Execute the generated pipeline\n   224→- Modify original skill/command/agent files\n   225→- Create new skills or agents\n   226→- Make architectural decisions about the workflow's logic\n   227→- Generate LLM content quality tests (only structural)\n   228→\n   229→## Related Commands\n   230→\n   231→- `/sc:implement` — General feature implementation\n   232→- `/sc:design` — System architecture design\n   233→- `/sc:analyze` — Code analysis for understanding workflows\n   234→- `/sc:build` — Build and compile after generating\n   235→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01MkLQv4vC6oFgW5qdoLhxSH","session_id":"b2ef0efa-f51b-45bb-8b9b-bca7a80178ed","uuid":"2d6bf805-22a7-43f6-b8f4-9b75a9611992"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_LpTjvtfrFhtNu6v4xcL4QGJd","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---\n     2→name: sc-cli-portify\n     3→description: \"Port inference-based SuperClaude workflows (commands + skills + agents) into programmatic CLI pipelines with sprint-style supervised execution. Use this skill when you need to convert any multi-step workflow into a reliable, repeatable CLI runner that uses Python to control flow and Claude to fill in content. Triggers on: portify, CLI pipeline, programmatic workflow, convert skill to CLI, workflow runner, deterministic pipeline, move to code, make repeatable, ensure consistency.\"\n     4→allowed-tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, TodoWrite, Task\n     5→---\n     6→\n     7→> **DEPRECATED**: This skill has been replaced by `sc-cli-portify-protocol/`.\n     8→> The protocol skill contains the full behavioral protocol with updated API-aligned refs.\n     9→> This directory is scheduled for removal after Phase 5 validation.\n    10→> Do NOT use this skill for new work — use `Skill sc:cli-portify-protocol` instead.\n    11→\n    12→# sc:cli-portify — Workflow-to-CLI Pipeline Compiler\n    13→\n    14→Convert inference-based SuperClaude workflows into programmatic CLI pipelines that ensure 100% completion and consistency between runs.\n    15→\n    16→## Purpose\n    17→\n    18→SuperClaude workflows implemented as commands + skills + agents + refs rely entirely on Claude's reasoning for orchestration. This works well for flexible, one-off tasks but breaks down for repeatable multi-step pipelines where you need:\n    19→\n    20→- **Deterministic control flow** — Python decides what runs when, not inference\n    21→- **Formal artifact validation** — Gates check output quality programmatically\n    22→- **Resume/retry** — Failed steps resume without re-running everything\n    23→- **Live monitoring** — TUI shows progress, stall detection, diagnostics\n    24→- **Budget economics** — Turn ledger tracks consumption across subprocesses\n    25→- **Consistent behavior** — Same inputs produce structurally identical outputs every run\n    26→\n    27→The existing `pipeline/` and `sprint/` modules already solve these problems. This skill bridges the gap by analyzing a workflow and generating a new CLI module that uses these proven patterns.\n    28→\n    29→## What Gets Generated\n    30→\n    31→A CLI subcommand package under `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`:\n    32→\n    33→| File | Purpose | When Generated |\n    34→|------|---------|----------------|\n    35→| `models.py` | Domain types extending `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult` | Always |\n    36→| `gates.py` | `GateCriteria` + semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]` | Always |\n    37→| `prompts.py` | LLM contract builders per Claude-assisted step | Always |\n    38→| `config.py` | CLI arg resolution, file discovery, config construction | Always |\n    39→| `executor.py` | Synchronous supervisor loop with `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism | Always |\n    40→| `monitor.py` | NDJSON incremental output parser with domain signals | Always |\n    41→| `process.py` | `ClaudeProcess` subclass with domain-specific prompt building | Always |\n    42→| `tui.py` | Rich live dashboard with gate state machine | Always |\n    43→| `logging_.py` | Dual JSONL + Markdown execution logging | Always |\n    44→| `diagnostics.py` | `DiagnosticCollector`, `FailureClassifier`, `ReportGenerator` | Always |\n    45→| `commands.py` | Click CLI group and subcommands | Always |\n    46→| `__init__.py` | Package exports | Always |\n    47→| `inventory.py` | Pure-programmatic file discovery/classification | If workflow has discovery steps |\n    48→| `filtering.py` | Pure-programmatic inter-step data filtering | If workflow has filtering between steps |\n    49→\n    50→Plus integration patches for `main.py`.\n    51→\n    52→## Required Input\n    53→\n    54→```\n    55→/sc:cli-portify --workflow <skill-name-or-path> [--name <cli-name>] [--output <dir>]\n    56→```\n    57→\n    58→| Argument | Required | Description |\n    59→|----------|----------|-------------|\n    60→| `--workflow` | Yes | Skill directory path or `sc-*` name to portify |\n    61→| `--name` | No | CLI subcommand name (default: derived from workflow) |\n    62→| `--output` | No | Output directory (default: `src/superclaude/cli/<name>/`) |\n    63→| `--dry-run` | No | Show decomposition plan without generating code |\n    64→| `--skip-integration` | No | Generate module only, skip main.py wiring |\n    65→\n    66→**STOP** if `--workflow` is not provided or the path doesn't resolve to a valid skill.\n    67→\n    68→## Behavioral Flow\n    69→\n    70→Four phases, each producing artifacts that feed the next. Present output to user for review after Phases 1 and 2 before continuing.\n    71→\n    72→### Phase 1: Workflow Analysis\n    73→\n    74→Load `refs/analysis-protocol.md` before this phase. It contains the discovery checklist, step decomposition algorithm, classification rubric, and output format template.\n    75→\n    76→**Goal**: Decompose the target workflow into a structured pipeline specification.\n    77→\n    78→1. **Discover components** — Find the command `.md`, skill `SKILL.md`, all `refs/` and `rules/`, all `templates/`, all `scripts/`, and all referenced agents. Build a component inventory table with paths, line counts, and purposes.\n    79→\n    80→2. **Map the protocol** — Extract the step-by-step behavioral flow. Pay attention to:\n    81→   - Wave/phase boundaries in the skill\n    82→   - Agent delegation patterns (which agent, parallel vs sequential)\n    83→   - Conditional execution paths (flags that skip steps)\n    84→   - Implicit inter-step data flow (what one step produces that another consumes)\n    85→\n    86→3. **Identify steps** — A new Step boundary exists when:\n    87→   - A new artifact is produced\n    88→   - A different agent takes over\n    89→   - Execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    90→   - A quality gate must be evaluated\n    91→   - Operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    92→\n    93→4. **Classify each step** on the programmatic spectrum:\n    94→   - **Pure programmatic**: Runs as a Python function, no Claude subprocess. Examples: file inventory via `git ls-files`, config construction, file filtering between passes, scoring formulas, structural validation, batch assignment. If the logic is deterministic and the input/output formats are well-defined, it should be programmatic.\n    95→   - **Claude-assisted**: Runs as a Claude subprocess with a prompt contract and gate. Examples: content analysis, writing reports, rubric-based assessment, creative synthesis, debate.\n    96→   - **Hybrid**: Programmatic setup → Claude subprocess → programmatic validation. Examples: steps that need Claude for content but have strict structural validation on the output.\n    97→\n    98→5. **Map dependencies and parallel groups** — Draw the data flow: which steps produce artifacts consumed by downstream steps? Which steps are independent and can run concurrently? For batched workflows, identify where batch parallelism applies.\n    99→\n   100→6. **Extract gates** — For each step output, identify what validation the original workflow expects. Map these to gate tiers: EXEMPT (informational), LIGHT (exists + non-empty), STANDARD (structural checks), STRICT (structural + semantic).\n   101→\n   102→7. **Assign gate modes** — `GateMode.BLOCKING` for steps whose output feeds downstream steps. `GateMode.TRAILING` for quality-only checks that don't affect data flow.\n   103→\n   104→**Output**: `portify-analysis.md` — Complete decomposition following the template in `refs/analysis-protocol.md`. Must include: component inventory, step graph with classifications, parallel groups, gates summary, data flow diagram, and recommendations.\n   105→\n   106→**Present to user for review before Phase 2.**\n   107→\n   108→### Phase 2: Pipeline Specification\n   109→\n   110→Load `refs/pipeline-spec.md` before this phase. It contains Step definition patterns, model design patterns, gate patterns, executor patterns, and the new unified-audit-gating patterns (TurnLedger, gate modes, subprocess isolation, context injection, resume semantics).\n   111→\n   112→**Goal**: Convert the analysis into concrete, code-ready specifications.\n   113→\n   114→1. **Design the Step graph** — Map each workflow step to `Step` objects. Batched parallel steps are `list[Step]` groups. Dynamic step counts (e.g., batch count determined at runtime) require a `build_steps()` function.\n   115→\n   116→2. **Define models** — Design domain-specific dataclasses:\n   117→   - Config extending `PipelineConfig` with workflow-specific fields (source paths, pass selection, batch sizes, stall/timeout policies)\n   118→   - Status enum with domain-specific states (e.g., `VALIDATION_FAIL`, `CRITICAL_FAIL` for audit workflows)\n   119→   - Result extending `StepResult` with execution telemetry\n   120→   - Aggregate result with `resume_command()` and `suggested_resume_budget`\n   121→   - Monitor state with domain-specific NDJSON signals\n   122→   - `TurnLedger` integration for budget tracking\n   123→\n   124→3. **Design prompts** — For each Claude-assisted step, write the prompt builder. Each prompt must specify:\n   125→   - What input files/context to embed (inline for <50KB, `--file` args for larger)\n   126→   - Required output sections and frontmatter fields\n   127→   - Machine-readable markers: `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE|HALT`\n   128→   - Any structural requirements the gate will check\n   129→\n   130→   If prompts collectively exceed ~300 lines, put them in a separate `portify-prompts.md`.\n   131→\n   132→4. **Design gates** — For each output artifact, define:\n   133→   - `GateCriteria` with tier, required frontmatter, min lines, semantic checks\n   134→   - `GateMode` (BLOCKING or TRAILING)\n   135→   - All semantic check functions returning `tuple[bool, str]`\n   136→\n   137→5. **Implement pure-programmatic steps** — Write actual runnable Python code for steps classified as \"pure programmatic\". These run as direct function calls in the executor, bypassing `ClaudeProcess`. Include: file discovery, domain classification, batch assignment, inter-pass filtering, config construction.\n   138→\n   139→6. **Design the executor** — Sprint-style synchronous supervisor:\n   140→   - `ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism (NOT async/await)\n   141→   - `time.sleep()` polling loops for monitoring\n   142→   - Per-step/batch monitoring with stall detection\n   143→   - `TurnLedger` budget guards before each launch\n   144→   - Runner-authored truth: reports from observed data, not Claude self-reporting\n   145→   - Subprocess isolation (4-layer model) for child Claude sessions\n   146→   - Context injection: prior results compressed into subsequent prompts\n   147→   - Signal-aware shutdown with `SignalHandler`\n   148→\n   149→7. **Plan integration** — Click command group, main.py import, file generation order.\n   150→\n   151→**Output**: `portify-spec.md` (+ optional `portify-prompts.md`) — Complete code spec with models, gates, executor design, pure-programmatic implementations, and integration plan.\n   152→\n   153→**Present to user for approval before Phase 3.**\n   154→\n   155→### Phase 3: Code Generation\n   156→\n   157→Load `refs/code-templates.md` before this phase. It contains parameterized file templates for every module.\n   158→\n   159→Generate files in dependency order (models → gates → prompts → config → monitor → process → executor → tui → logging → diagnostics → commands → __init__). Each file imports from `superclaude.cli.pipeline` for shared base types and follows sprint/roadmap naming conventions.\n   160→\n   161→### Phase 4: Integration\n   162→\n   163→1. Patch `main.py` — Add import and `app.add_command()`\n   164→2. Verify imports — Quick check for circular dependencies\n   165→3. Generate structural test — Validates step graph, gate definitions, and model consistency\n   166→4. Write `portify-summary.md` — File inventory, CLI usage, step graph, known limitations\n   167→\n   168→## Decision Framework: What Becomes Programmatic\n   169→\n   170→The value of portification is moving orchestration out of inference. Here are concrete examples from tested workflows:\n   171→\n   172→| Operation | Classification | Why |\n   173→|-----------|---------------|-----|\n   174→| Enumerate repo files | Pure programmatic | `git ls-files` + pattern matching is deterministic |\n   175→| Classify file domains | Pure programmatic | Extension/path pattern rules, no judgment needed |\n   176→| Assign files to batches | Pure programmatic | Math: divide files by batch_size, assign to agents |\n   177→| Filter Pass 1 results for Pass 2 | Pure programmatic | Parse markdown, extract classifications, filter list |\n   178→| Analyze file for issues | Claude-assisted | Requires reading code, understanding context |\n   179→| Score variant quality | Hybrid | Formulas are programmatic, rubric evaluation is Claude |\n   180→| Validate structural integrity | Pure programmatic | Regex checks, section presence, field counts |\n   181→| Spot-check verification | Claude-assisted | Re-reads files, re-evaluates claims independently |\n   182→| Merge batch reports | Claude-assisted | Requires synthesis, deduplication, pattern extraction |\n   183→| Compute convergence score | Pure programmatic | `agreed / total` — arithmetic |\n   184→| Generate executive summary | Claude-assisted | Requires narrative synthesis |\n   185→\n   186→### Rule of Thumb\n   187→If you can write a Python function for it with clear input→output types and no ambiguity, make it programmatic. If it requires reading natural language, making judgments, or synthesizing information, use Claude with a gate to verify the output.\n   188→\n   189→## Code Generation Principles\n   190→\n   191→These align with the unified-audit-gating architecture in `pipeline/`.\n   192→\n   193→### Critical Constraints\n   194→1. **Synchronous execution** — Sprint uses `threading` + `time.sleep()` polling. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for batch parallelism. Do NOT use async/await.\n   195→2. **Gate function signatures** — All semantic checks return `tuple[bool, str]`. The reason string feeds diagnostics and retry decisions.\n   196→3. **Runner-authored truth** — Reports derive from runner-observed data (exit codes, artifacts, gates, monitor signals), not Claude's self-reported status.\n   197→4. **Deterministic flow control** — Python makes all decisions about what runs next. Claude never decides \"what's next\".\n   198→\n   199→### Shared Pipeline Patterns\n   200→5. **Reuse `pipeline/` primitives** — `PipelineConfig`, `Step`, `StepResult`, `GateMode`, `GateCriteria`, `SemanticCheck`, `gate_passed()`, `ClaudeProcess`\n   201→6. **Gate modes** — `BLOCKING` (default) stops pipeline on failure. `TRAILING` defers evaluation. Use trailing only for quality checks that don't affect downstream data flow.\n   202→7. **Turn budget** — `TurnLedger` with debit/credit/guard for multi-subprocess pipelines. Pre-launch guards prevent launching when budget too low.\n   203→8. **Subprocess isolation** — 4-layer model: scoped work dir, git ceiling, isolated plugin dir, isolated settings dir.\n   204→9. **Context injection** — Prior results serialized into subsequent prompts. Compress older, preserve recent.\n   205→10. **Resume-first failures** — On halt: exact resume command, remaining work, diagnostic path, budget suggestion.\n   206→11. **Diagnostic chain** — Runner-side failure analysis that doesn't consume turn budget.\n   207→\n   208→### Output Guidance\n   209→- `portify-analysis.md`: Under 400 lines. Step graph, gates, data flow.\n   210→- `portify-spec.md`: Split prompts to separate file if spec exceeds 800 lines.\n   211→- Pure-programmatic steps: Full implementation code, not descriptions.\n   212→\n   213→## Boundaries\n   214→\n   215→### Will Do\n   216→- Analyze any SuperClaude skill/command/agent workflow\n   217→- Decompose into structured pipeline specification\n   218→- Generate complete CLI subcommand module\n   219→- Wire module into CLI infrastructure\n   220→- Generate structural tests for pipeline\n   221→\n   222→### Will Not Do\n   223→- Execute the generated pipeline\n   224→- Modify original skill/command/agent files\n   225→- Create new skills or agents\n   226→- Make architectural decisions about the workflow's logic\n   227→- Generate LLM content quality tests (only structural)\n   228→\n   229→## Related Commands\n   230→\n   231→- `/sc:implement` — General feature implementation\n   232→- `/sc:design` — System architecture design\n   233→- `/sc:analyze` — Code analysis for understanding workflows\n   234→- `/sc:build` — Build and compile after generating\n   235→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01MkLQv4vC6oFgW5qdoLhxSH","session_id":"b2ef0efa-f51b-45bb-8b9b-bca7a80178ed","uuid":"d87eea32-8375-4f60-ac8b-800aedf985ff"}

Component Inventory Engine (FR-015)\n    11→\n    12→### ID Assignment Algorithm\n    13→\n    14→```\n    15→assign_component_ids(workflow: ResolvedWorkflow) -> list[Component]\n    16→```\n    17→\n    18→Components are assigned stable `component_id` values in format `C-NNN`:\n    19→\n    20→1. **Ordering**: Components are sorted deterministically:\n    21→   - Command `.md` first (C-001)\n    22→   - Skill `SKILL.md` second (C-002)\n    23→   - Refs sorted alphabetically (C-003, C-004, ...)\n    24→   - Rules sorted alphabetically (next IDs)\n    25→   - Templates sorted alphabetically (next IDs)\n    26→   - Scripts sorted alphabetically (next IDs)\n    27→   - Agents sorted alphabetically (final IDs)\n    28→\n    29→2. **Stability**: The ID assignment is deterministic — same input always produces same IDs. IDs are assigned by position in the sorted list, zero-padded to 3 digits.\n    30→\n    31→3. **Output**: Each component record:\n    32→   ```yaml\n    33→   component_id: \"C-001\"\n    34→   path: \"src/superclaude/commands/cleanup-audit.md\"\n    35→   type: \"command\"\n    36→   line_count: 45\n    37→   purpose: \"Slash command entry point for cleanup audit\"\n    38→   ```\n    39→\n    40→### Test Execution: sc-cleanup-audit-protocol\n    41→\n    42→| component_id | Path | Type | Lines | Purpose |\n    43→|-------------|------|------|-------|---------|\n    44→| C-001 | `commands/cleanup-audit.md` | command | ~45 | Command entry point |\n    45→| C-002 | `skills/sc-cleanup-audit-protocol/SKILL.md` | skill | ~800 | Full protocol |\n    46→| C-003 | `skills/sc-cleanup-audit-protocol/refs/...` | ref | varies | Reference files |\n    47→| ... | (sorted alphabetically) | ... | ... | ... |\n    48→\n    49→**Result**: All components assigned unique C-NNN IDs ✅\n    50→\n    51→---\n    52→\n    53→## 2. Step Decomposition Engine (FR-016, FR-049)\n    54→\n    55→### ID Assignment Algorithm\n    56→\n    57→```\n    58→decompose_steps(skill_md: str, analysis_protocol: str) -> list[SourceStep]\n    59→```\n    60→\n    61→Steps are identified by applying the boundary detection rules from `refs/analysis-protocol.md`:\n    62→\n    63→**A new Step starts when**:\n    64→- A new artifact is produced\n    65→- A different agent takes over\n    66→- The execution mode changes (sequential → parallel)\n    67→- A quality gate must be evaluated\n    68→- The operation type changes (analysis → generation → validation)\n    69→\n    70→Steps are assigned stable `source_id` values in format `S-NNN`:\n    71→\n    72→1. **Ordering**: Steps are numbered in the order they appear in the workflow's behavioral flow\n    73→2. **Stability**: Same workflow text always produces same step boundaries and IDs\n    74→3. **Conservation Invariant**: `|source_steps| == |classified_steps|`\n    75→   - Every identified step MUST receive exactly one classification\n    76→   - No steps may be added or removed during classification\n    77→   - This invariant is checked after classification and recorded in the contract\n    78→\n    79→### Conservation Invariant Enforcement\n    80→\n    81→```\n    82→enforce_conservation(source_steps: list, classified_steps: list) -> ConservationResult\n    83→```\n    84→\n    85→```yaml\n    86→conservation_invariant:\n    87→  source_step_count: N       # Number of steps identified by decomposition\n    88→  classified_step_count: N   # Number of steps after classification\n    89→  invariant_holds: true      # source_step_count == classified_step_count\n    90→```\n    91→\n    92→If invariant fails: **blocking error** — classification modified the step list. This is a bug in the classification engine.\n    93→\n    94→### Test Execution: sc-cleanup-audit-protocol\n    95→\n    96→The cleanup-audit workflow has a multi-pass audit structure (pattern from analysis-protocol.md):\n    97→\n    98→| source_id | Name | Boundary Trigger |\n    99→|-----------|------|-----------------|\n   100→| S-001 | Discover and classify files | New artifact (file inventory) |\n   101→| S-002 | Surface scan (Pass 1) — batch | Agent change (audit-scanner) + parallel |\n   102→| S-003 | Structural analysis (Pass 2) — batch | Agent change (audit-analyzer) |\n   103→| S-004 | Cross-cutting comparison (Pass 3) | Agent change (audit-comparator) + mode change |\n   104→| S-005 | Consolidate findings | Agent change (audit-consolidator) |\n   105→| S-006 | Validate claims (spot-check) | Agent change (audit-validator) |\n   106→\n   107→**source_step_count**: 6\n   108→**classified_step_count**: 6\n   109→**Conservation invariant**: `6 == 6` → HOLDS ✅\n   110→\n   111→---\n   112→\n   113→## 3. Step Classification Engine (FR-017)\n   114→\n   115→### Classification Algorithm\n   116→\n   117→```\n   118→classify_steps(source_steps: list[SourceStep]) -> list[ClassifiedStep]\n   119→```\n   120→\n   121→Each step is classified as `pure_programmatic`, `claude_assisted`, or `hybrid` with a confidence score:\n   122→\n   123→| Classification | Criteria | Confidence Factors |\n   124→|---------------|---------|-------------------|\n   125→| `pure_programmatic` | Deterministic, formula-based, structural; uses only Python/bash tools; no natural language judgment | High (0.9+): file listing, math, regex |\n   126→| `claude_assisted` | Content generation, analysis, judgment; requires reading/synthesizing text | High (0.9+): writing reports, rubric evaluation |\n   127→| `hybrid` | Programmatic setup → Claude execution → programmatic validation | Medium (0.7-0.9): structured analysis with validation |\n   128→\n   129→**Confidence scoring factors**:\n   130→- Tool list analysis: if step only uses Read/Grep/Glob/Bash → +0.2 toward programmatic\n   131→- Output analysis: if output is markdown report → +0.2 toward claude_assisted\n   132→- Agent delegation: if step delegates to a Claude agent → +0.3 toward claude_assisted\n   133→- Structural validation: if step has strict gate with semantic checks → +0.1 toward hybrid\n   134→- Determinism: if step could produce different outputs for same input → +0.2 toward claude_assisted\n   135→\n   136→**User review flag**: Steps with confidence < 0.7 are flagged for user review before Phase 2 proceeds.\n   137→\n   138→### Test Execution: sc-cleanup-audit-protocol\n   139→\n   140→| source_id | Name | Classification | Confidence | Flagged? |\n   141→|-----------|------|---------------|------------|----------|\n   142→| S-001 | Discover and classify files | `pure_programmatic` | 0.95 | No |\n   143→| S-002 | Surface scan (Pass 1) | `claude_assisted` | 0.90 | No |\n   144→| S-003 | Structural analysis (Pass 2) | `claude_assisted` | 0.90 | No |\n   145→| S-004 | Cross-cutting comparison | `claude_assisted` | 0.85 | No |\n   146→| S-005 | Consolidate findings | `claude_assisted` | 0.88 | No |\n   147→| S-006 | Validate claims (spot-check) | `claude_assisted` | 0.85 | No |\n   148→\n   149→**Classification summary**:\n   150→- Pure programmatic: 1 step (S-001)\n   151→- Claude-assisted: 5 steps (S-002 through S-006)\n   152→- Hybrid: 0 steps\n   153→- Flagged for review: 0 steps (all confidence ≥ 0.7)\n   154→\n   155→---\n   156→\n   157→## 4. Acceptance Criteria Verification\n   158→\n   159→| Criterion | Status |\n   160→|-----------|--------|\n   161→| Component inventory assigns unique C-NNN IDs to all detected components in test workflow | ✅ PASS |\n   162→| Step decomposition assigns unique S-NNN IDs with conservation invariant holding: `\\|source_steps\\| == \\|classified_steps\\|` | ✅ PASS (6 == 6) |\n   163→| Classification engine produces pure_programmatic/claude_assisted/hybrid labels with confidence scores | ✅ PASS |\n   164→| Steps with confidence < 0.7 flagged for user review | ✅ PASS (no flags needed — all above 0.7) |\n   165→"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01MkLQv4vC6oFgW5qdoLhxSH","session_id":"b2ef0efa-f51b-45bb-8b9b-bca7a80178ed","uuid":"b52ae706-2952-48c3-b775-057e715cf0e2"}

Light gate verifies output file exists and is non-empty.\n   158→\n   159→#### G-002: Surface scan — Pass 1 (STANDARD / BLOCKING)\n   160→\n   161→```python\n   162→G002_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   163→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\", \"pass\"],\n   164→    min_lines=50,\n   165→    enforcement_tier=\"STANDARD\",\n   166→    semantic_checks=[\n   167→        SemanticCheck(\n   168→            name=\"has_classification_table\",\n   169→            check_fn=_has_classification_table,\n   170→            failure_message=\"Surface scan output missing file classification table\",\n   171→        ),\n   172→    ],\n   173→)\n   174→```\n   175→\n   176→```python\n   177→def _has_classification_table(content: str) -> bool:\n   178→    \"\"\"Verify surface scan contains a file classification table with DELETE/REVIEW/KEEP.\"\"\"\n   179→    return any(\n   180→        keyword in content\n   181→        for keyword in [\"DELETE\", \"REVIEW\", \"KEEP\"]\n   182→    ) and \"|\" in content\n   183→```\n   184→\n   185→#### G-003: Structural analysis — Pass 2 (STANDARD / BLOCKING)\n   186→\n   187→```python\n   188→G003_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   189→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\", \"pass\"],\n   190→    min_lines=50,\n   191→    enforcement_tier=\"STANDARD\",\n   192→    semantic_checks=[\n   193→        SemanticCheck(\n   194→            name=\"has_per_file_profiles\",\n   195→            check_fn=_has_per_file_profiles,\n   196→            failure_message=\"Structural analysis missing per-file profile sections\",\n   197→        ),\n   198→    ],\n   199→)\n   200→```\n   201→\n   202→```python\n   203→def _has_per_file_profiles(content: str) -> bool:\n   204→    \"\"\"Verify structural analysis contains per-file profiles with 8-field format.\"\"\"\n   205→    return content.count(\"##\") >= 3 and \"profile\" in content.lower()\n   206→```\n   207→\n   208→#### G-004: Cross-cutting comparison — Pass 3 (STRICT / BLOCKING)\n   209→\n   210→```python\n   211→G004_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   212→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\", \"pass\", \"finding_count\"],\n   213→    min_lines=100,\n   214→    enforcement_tier=\"STRICT\",\n   215→    semantic_checks=[\n   216→        SemanticCheck(\n   217→            name=\"has_cross_cutting_findings\",\n   218→            check_fn=_has_cross_cutting_findings,\n   219→            failure_message=\"Cross-cutting comparison missing duplication/sprawl findings\",\n   220→        ),\n   221→        SemanticCheck(\n   222→            name=\"has_consolidation_opportunities\",\n   223→            check_fn=_has_consolidation_opportunities,\n   224→            failure_message=\"Cross-cutting comparison missing consolidation opportunity section\",\n   225→        ),\n   226→    ],\n   227→)\n   228→```\n   229→\n   230→```python\n   231→def _has_cross_cutting_findings(content: str) -> bool:\n   232→    \"\"\"Verify cross-cutting analysis contains duplication or sprawl findings.\"\"\"\n   233→    lower = content.lower()\n   234→    return \"duplicat\" in lower or \"sprawl\" in lower or \"consolidat\" in lower\n   235→\n   236→def _has_consolidation_opportunities(content: str) -> bool:\n   237→    \"\"\"Verify consolidation opportunities section exists.\"\"\"\n   238→    return \"consolidat\" in content.lower() and \"##\" in content\n   239→```\n   240→\n   241→#### G-005: Consolidate findings (STRICT / BLOCKING)\n   242→\n   243→```python\n   244→G005_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   245→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\", \"total_findings\", \"severity_distribution\"],\n   246→    min_lines=100,\n   247→    enforcement_tier=\"STRICT\",\n   248→    semantic_checks=[\n   249→        SemanticCheck(\n   250→            name=\"has_deduplication_evidence\",\n   251→            check_fn=_has_deduplication_evidence,\n   252→            failure_message=\"Consolidated report missing deduplication evidence\",\n   253→        ),\n   254→        SemanticCheck(\n   255→            name=\"has_exit_recommendation\",\n   256→            check_fn=_has_exit_recommendation,\n   257→            failure_message=\"Consolidated report missing EXIT_RECOMMENDATION marker\",\n   258→        ),\n   259→    ],\n   260→)\n   261→```\n   262→\n   263→```python\n   264→def _has_deduplication_evidence(content: str) -> bool:\n   265→    \"\"\"Verify consolidated report shows deduplication was performed.\"\"\"\n   266→    return \"dedup\" in content.lower() or \"merged\" in content.lower()\n   267→\n   268→def _has_exit_recommendation(content: str) -> bool:\n   269→    \"\"\"Verify EXIT_RECOMMENDATION marker is present.\"\"\"\n   270→    return \"EXIT_RECOMMENDATION:\" in content\n   271→```\n   272→\n   273→#### G-006: Validate claims — spot-check (STANDARD / TRAILING)\n   274→\n   275→```python\n   276→G006_GATE = GateCriteria(\n   277→    required_frontmatter_fields=[\"title\", \"status\"],\n   278→    min_lines=30,\n   279→    enforcement_tier=\"STANDARD\",\n   280→    semantic_checks=[\n   281→        SemanticCheck(\n   282→            name=\"has_validation_verdicts\",\n   283→            check_fn=_has_validation_verdicts,\n   284→            failure_message=\"Spot-check output missing validation verdicts (CONFIRMED/REFUTED)\",\n   285→        ),\n   286→    ],\n   287→)\n   288→```\n   289→\n   290→```python\n   291→def _has_validation_verdicts(content: str) -> bool:\n   292→    \"\"\"Verify spot-check contains CONFIRMED or REFUTED verdicts.\"\"\"\n   293→    upper = content.upper()\n   294→    return \"CONFIRMED\" in upper or \"REFUTED\" in upper\n   295→```\n   296→\n   297→### 2.2 Gate Field Cross-Reference\n   298→\n models\n- `diagnostics.py`: imports models\n- `commands.py`: imports config and executor\n- `__init__.py`: re-exports command group\n\n## What each file is supposed to contain\n\n### 1) `models.py`\nPurpose:\n- Domain model layer for the generated pipeline.\n\nExpected contents:\n- Domain-specific status enum, typically extending sprint-style states:\n  - `PENDING`\n  - `RUNNING`\n  - `PASS`\n  - `PASS_NO_SIGNAL`\n  - `PASS_NO_REPORT`\n  - `INCOMPLETE`\n  - `HALT`\n  - `TIMEOUT`\n  - `ERROR`\n  - `SKIPPED`\n- Aggregate outcome enum, typically:\n  - `SUCCESS`\n  - `HALTED`\n  - `INTERRUPTED`\n  - `ERROR`\n- Optional workflow/domain enums\n- Config dataclass extending `PipelineConfig`\n- Step subclass extending `Step`\n- Step result dataclass extending `StepResult`\n- Aggregate pipeline result model\n- Monitor state dataclass\n\nExpected key classes/functions/properties:\n- `{PREFIX}Status(Enum)`\n- `{PREFIX}Outcome(Enum)`\n- `{PREFIX}Config(PipelineConfig)`\n- `{PREFIX}Step(Step)`\n- `{PREFIX}StepResult(StepResult)`\n- `{PREFIX}Result`\n- `{PREFIX}MonitorState`\n- `resume_command()`\n- computed properties like:\n  - `duration_seconds`\n  - `steps_passed`\n  - `steps_failed`\n  - config path properties\n\nNotable requirements:\n- Config should hold workflow-specific fields such as source path, pass selection, batch sizes, stall policies.\n- Result model should support resume semantics and suggested resume budget.\n- Monitor state should hold NDJSON-derived runtime signals.\n\n### 2) `gates.py`\nPurpose:\n- Programmatic validation of step outputs.\n\nExpected contents:\n- Gate definitions for each output artifact\n- Semantic check functions\n- Registry of all gates\n\nExpected key elements:\n- `GateCriteria` objects per step/output\n- `SemanticCheck` entries\n- `ALL_GATES: dict[str, GateCriteria]`\n- semantic check functions like:\n  - heading validation\n  - section presence\n  - cross-reference checks\n  - structural field checks\n\nImportant requirement:\n- The protocol skill says semantic checks should return `tuple[bool, str]` because the reason string feeds diagnostics/retries.\n- However, parts of `pipeline-spec.md` and `code-templates.md` still show older `Callable[[str], bool]` wording.\n- So there is a spec inconsistency:\n  - authoritative behavioral text: `tuple[bool, str]`\n  - some examples/templates: `bool`\n- Since the protocol repeatedly emphasizes reason strings, the intended current requirement appears to be `tuple[bool, str]`.\n\n### 3) `prompts.py`\nPurpose:\n- Build prompt contracts for each Claude-assisted step.\n\nExpected contents:\n- One prompt builder function per Claude-assisted step\n- Optional depth instruction constants\n- Input embedding logic for small vs large files\n\nExpected key functions:\n- `build_<step>_prompt(...) -> str`\n- possible helper similar to `_embed_inputs(...)`\n\nPrompt requirements:\n- Specify embedded context or file args\n- Require explicit output structure\n- Require frontmatter fields\n- Require machine-readable markers:\n  - `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: CONTINUE`\n  - `EXIT_RECOMMENDATION: HALT`\n- Encode structural expectations the gate will verify\n\nIf prompts are large:\n- split into a separate `portify-prompts.md` during spec phase\n\n### 4) `config.py`\nPurpose:\n- Resolve CLI arguments into a validated config object.\n\nExpected contents:\n- Config loader\n- path resolution\n- defaults\n- validation\n- possibly workflow-specific discovery helpers\n\nExpected key functions:\n- `load_<cli_name>_config(...) -> {PREFIX}Config`\n- helper functions for:\n  - input validation\n  - default path derivation\n  - file discovery\n  - CLI argument normalization\n\nRequirements mentioned in protocol:\n- CLI arg resolution\n- file discovery\n- config construction\n- validation logic\n- default output path behavior is clarified in:\n  - `/config/workspace/SuperClaude_Framework/.claude/skills/sc-cli-portify-protocol/decisions.yaml`\n\n### 5) `monitor.py`\nPurpose:\n- Live monitoring of Claude subprocess output.\n\nExpected contents:\n- Incremental NDJSON output parser\n- signal extraction\n- growth/stall tracking\n- max-turns error detection\n\nExpected key classes/functions:\n- `OutputMonitor`\n- `detect_error_max_turns(...)`\n- state-reset/start/stop/get_state methods\n\nRequirements:\n- Follow sprint monitor pattern closely\n- Adapt only signal extraction to the target domain\n- Feed runtime state into TUI and executor stall logic\n\n### 6) `process.py`\nPurpose:\n- Domain-specific Claude subprocess wrapper.\n\nExpected contents:\n- subclass of shared pipeline `ClaudeProcess`\n- domain-specific prompt assembly\n- signal handling or process wrapper utilities\n\nExpected key classes/functions:\n- `{PREFIX}Process(ClaudeProcess)`\n- `build_prompt()`\n- `SignalHandler`\n\nRequirements:\n- use sprint process pattern\n- construct prompt with proper task framing\n- support subprocess isolation patterns\n- likely integrate context injection from earlier step results\n\n### 7) `executor.py`\nPurpose:\n- The orchestration brain of the generated pipeline.\n\nExpected contents:\n- synchronous supervisor loop\n- step graph builder\n- launch/monitor/wait/classify cycle\n- gate evaluation\n- diagnostics handoff\n- halt/resume logic\n- optional routing for pure-programmatic steps\n\nExpected key functions:\n- `execute_<cli_name>(config) -> {PREFIX}Result`\n- `_build_steps(config)`\n- `_determine_status(exit_code, step, config)`\n- possibly `execute_step(...)`\n- possibly `run_programmatic_step(...)`\n- batch parallel execution using `ThreadPoolExecutor`\n\nCritical requirements:\n- synchronous only; no `asyncio`\n- `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism\n- `time.sleep()` polling loops\n- per-step monitoring and stall detection\n- turn-budget guards with `TurnLedger`\n- runner-authored truth\n- subprocess isolation\n- context injection\n- resume-first failure behavior\n\n### 8) `tui.py`\nPurpose:\n- Rich live dashboard during pipeline execution.\n\nExpected contents:\n- live display\n- step table\n- progress bar\n- active step details\n- gate display states/styles\n\nExpected key classes/elements:\n- `{PREFIX}TUI`\n- gate display state machine, likely with states like:\n  - `NONE`\n  - `CHECKING`\n  - `PASS`\n  - `FAIL_DEFERRED`\n  - `REMEDIATING`\n  - `REMEDIATED`\n  - `HALT`\n\nRequirements:\n- follow sprint TUI pattern\n- show monitoring state, progress, current step, gate state\n\n### 9) `logging_.py`\nPurpose:\n- Durable execution logs.\n\nExpected contents:\n- JSONL log writer\n- Markdown log writer\n- screen/log sync\n\nExpected key classes/functions:\n- `{PREFIX}Logger`\n- methods like:\n  - `write_header()`\n  - `write_step_start(step)`\n  - `write_step_result(result)`\n  - `write_summary(result)`\n\nRequirements:\n- dual JSONL + Markdown logging\n- align with sprint logging pattern\n\n### 10) `diagnostics.py`\nPurpose:\n- Failure analysis after step or gate problems.\n\nExpected contents:\n- failure bundle collection\n- classification\n- report generation\n\nExpected key classes:\n- `DiagnosticCollector`\n- `FailureClassifier`\n- `ReportGenerator`\n\nRequirements:\n- runner-side diagnostics only\n- should not consume Claude turn budget\n- adapt failure categories to the target pipeline domain\n\n### 11) `commands.py`\nPurpose:\n- Click entrypoints for the generated CLI subcommand.\n\nExpected contents:\n- Click group\n- `run` command\n- options and arguments\n- dry-run renderer\n\nExpected key functions:\n- `@click.group(\"{CLI_NAME}\")`\n- `{GROUP_NAME}_group()`\n- `run(...)`\n- `_print_dry_run(config)`\n\nRequirements:\n- imports config loader and executor\n- exits with success/failure based on aggregate result\n- should expose workflow-specific CLI options\n\n### 12) `__init__.py`\nPurpose:\n- Package export surface.\n\nExpected contents:\n- import of the Click group\n- `__all__`\n\nExpected key export:\n- `{GROUP_NAME}_group`\n\n## Shared base classes and pipeline primitives the generated files rely on\n\nThe generated module is expected to reuse shared infrastructure from `superclaude.cli.pipeline`, especially:\n- `PipelineConfig`\n- `Step`\n- `StepResult`\n- `GateMode`\n- `GateCriteria`\n- `SemanticCheck`\n- `gate_passed()`\n- requirements\n\nThe portify spec expects workflow operations to be expressed as:\n- single `Step` objects for sequential work\n- `list[Step]` groups for parallel work\n- dynamic `build_steps()` when
